one_line_takeaway: A small-scale empirical benchmarking study (not a security paper) finding that DaRE's exact SISA-based unlearning keeps Random Forest prediction accuracy perfectly stable but is, contrary to the authors' own hypothesis, slower than naive full retraining on their HSpam14 subsets.
key_finding: Hypothesis H2 (SISA/DaRE would beat Naive Retraining on Computational Cost while matching Consistency) was refuted by the authors' own experiment: DaRE's Exact Unlearning via SISA was, on average, computationally slower than Naive Retraining (Claim 1), attributed to the overhead of DaRE's tree-rebalancing to preserve prediction consistency. Consistency itself was perfectly stable for SISA/DaRE -- percent change in Consistency was exactly zero across all 9 SISA trials -- whereas Naive Retraining's Consistency changes were noisy and non-monotonic across training-set sizes (e.g., at 25% deletion, percent-change rose from n=10 to n=100 but turned negative at n=1000). Both methods' raw Consistency scores fell within 0.70-0.84 (Claim 2), with DaRE/SISA's pre-deletion baseline accuracy slightly higher than Naive Retraining's. The authors conclude SISA/DaRE is preferable for large datasets despite its Computational Cost penalty, while Naive Retraining is only viable for small datasets.
core_contribution: An exploratory, single-author empirical comparison of two Machine Unlearning approaches -- Naive Retraining (full model retrain after deletion) and Exact Unlearning via the SISA framework (implemented as DaRE, a removal-enabled Random Forest from Brophy & Lowd 2020) -- measured on subsets of the HSpam14 tweet-spam dataset. The paper runs 18 total trials (9 per method) crossing three training-set sizes (n=10, 100, 1000) with three deletion percentages (25%, 50%, 75%), scoring each on 'Consistency' (test-set prediction accuracy before vs. after deletion) and 'Computational Cost' (wall-clock time of the delete+retrain/refit operation). It also proposes, as pure future-work brainstorming (not implemented or evaluated), a conceptual 'PUMU' framework that would combine Positive-Unlabeled Learning with Machine Unlearning.
threat_model: None in the paper's own contribution. There is no adversary, attacker capability, or privacy-leakage measurement (e.g., no membership-inference or reconstruction attack before/after unlearning). Section IV-I (Chen2025) surveys unlearning-specific attack categories from a cited related-work paper -- misclassification attacks, adversarial-robustness attacks, backdoor-injection attacks, fairness attacks, explanation attacks, and running-time attacks -- but this is a summary of someone else's survey, not a threat model the authors apply, extend, or evaluate against their own DaRE/SISA experiments. The paper's actual framing is regulatory/ethical compliance (GDPR/CCPA 'right to be forgotten'), not adversarial security.
limitations: (1) Experimental scale is tiny relative to the touted 14-million-tweet HSpam14 dataset -- actual training sets used are n=10, 100, and 1000, so results may not generalize to realistic production-scale unlearning workloads. (2) No stated repetition/averaging over multiple random seeds for the randomly-selected deletion indices, so the reported accuracy swings (including negative and reversing trends across the three sizes) could reflect small-sample variance rather than a genuine algorithmic trend. (3) Only one unlearning-capable model family (Random Forest via DaRE) is tested; the paper's own 'Model Complexity' challenge (deep neural networks resist exact unlearning) is explicitly named as unaddressed. (4) Despite the privacy framing, no privacy-specific metric is measured -- there is no membership-inference test, no measurement of residual information leakage after deletion, and no formal (e.g., differential-privacy) guarantee evaluated. (5) The PUMU framework (Sec. VIII) is conceptual only -- not implemented, not evaluated, and offered as future-research brainstorming. (6) Single-author, coursework-style report scope with a narrow literature review (two corpora queried with one search string) used mainly to select DaRE over alternatives like HedgeCut on convenience/learning-curve grounds rather than a rigorous comparative criterion.

datasets_used: HSpam14 (Sedhai & Sun 2015; described as 14 million annotated tweets used for hashtag-oriented spam research, though experiments used training subsets of only n=10, 100, and 1000 elements)
quantitative_results: 18 total trials run: 9 for Naive Retraining and 9 for Exact Unlearning via SISA, crossing training-set sizes n={10,100,1000} with deletion percentages %={0.25,0.50,0.75}
Percent Change in Consistency = 0 for all 9 Exact Unlearning via SISA (DaRE) trials (Consistency identical before and after deletion)
Consistency (accuracy) for both methods bounded within [0.70, 0.84] inclusive across all trials (Claim 2)
At 25% deletion, Naive Retraining's Percent Change in Consistency rose from n=10 to n=100 but became negative at n=1000
At 50% deletion, Naive Retraining's Percent Change in Consistency decreased monotonically as training-set size increased from 10 to 1000, but stayed positive
At 75% deletion, Computational Cost for Naive Retraining ordered ascending as: n=100 < n=10 < n=1000
Exact Unlearning via SISA's Computational Cost increased both with higher deletion percentage and with larger training-set size
On average, Exact Unlearning via SISA had higher Computational Cost than Naive Retraining (Claim 1), refuting hypothesis H2
Pre-deletion (baseline) Consistency slightly favored Exact Unlearning via SISA/DaRE over Naive Retraining
baselines_compared: Naive Retraining (standard scikit-learn-style Random Forest Classifier, deleted elements then fully retrained from scratch)
Exact Unlearning via SISA using DaRE (Data Removal-Enabled Random Forest, Brophy & Lowd 2020 implementation, dare_rf library)
